attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old Indigenous woman presents to the Emergency Department with episodic chest pain over the past week. The pain is worse when breathing in and improves when leaning forward. She reports intermittent joint pain affecting her left knee, left hand and right wrist. She has a medical history of chronic mouth ulcers, hypertension and depression. Her medications include perindopril and sertraline. She has a 10-pack-year smoking history.

On examination, she appears uncomfortable. Her pulse rate is 77/min, blood pressure 138/92 mmHg, respiratory rate 17/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.1°C. She has a scarred facial rash across her face and scalp. Heart sounds are dual with an audible rub. Chest is clear to auscultation.

Her investigation results include:

  • Hb: 102g/L (115 – 165)

  • WCC: 3.4 x 109/L (3.6 – 11.0)

  • RCC: 4.5 x 109​​​​​​/L (3.8 – 5.8)

  • Platelet count: 128 x109/L (140 – 400)

  • ANA: 1:320, homogenous pattern

  • Urinalysis is unremarkable
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient most likely has systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). SLE explains her chest pain (secondary to pericarditis), her polyarthralgia, her chronic mouth ulcers and her scarred facial rash. Her risk factors include being a female of childbearing age, being a smoker, and being Indigenous. A positive ANA result supports the classification of SLE, as does thrombocytopenia, leukopenia, and normocytic anaemia (secondary to chronic inflammation). The 2019 EULAR/ACR Classification Criteria for Systemic Lupus Erythematosus requires the following to classify a patient as having SLE:

  1. Entry criteria: ANA at a titre above 1:80

  2. Additive criteria, which has i) clinical domain and ii) immunological domain.

Clinical domains include:

  • Fever (2 points)

  • Leukopenia (3 points)

  • Thrombocytopenia (4 points)

  • Autoimmune haemolysis (4 points)

  • Delirium (2 points)

  • Psychosis (3 points)

  • Seizure (5 points)

  • Non-scarring alopecia (2 points)

  • Oral ulcers (2 points)

  • Subacute cutaneous or discoid lupus (4 points)

  • Acute cutaneous lupus (6 points)

  • Pleural or pericardial effusion (5 points)

  • Acute pericarditis (6 points)

  • Joint involvement (6 points)

  • Proteinuria >0.5g/ day (4 points)

  • Renal biopsy Class II or V lupus nephritis (8 points)

  • Renal biopsy Class III or IV lupus nephritis (10 points)

Positive laboratory results include:

  • Anticardiolipin antibodies or Anti-beta2GP1 antibodies or lupus anticoagulant (2 points)

  • Low C3 or low C4 (3 points)

  • Low C3 and low C4 (4 points)

  • Anti-dsDNA antibody or Anti-Smith antibody (6 points)

This patient has a total score of at least 23 points (6 for pericarditis, 6 for joint involvement, 2 for thrombocytopenia, 2 for oral ulcers, 3 for leukopenia, 4 for thrombocytopenia). To potentially help with the diagnosis, it would be worthwhile to also test for SLE-specific antibodies such as Anti-dsDNA antibody and Anti-Smith antibody (especially based on the ANA homogenous pattern which suggest post Anti-dsDNA antibodies).

This patient is less likely to have Sjogren's syndrome as it has less of the clinical manifestations, and a speckled pattern on ANA would be expected, indicative of a likely positive ENA. Dry eyes and dry mouth are examples of symptoms that commonly emerge with Sjogren's syndrome, but are not present for this patient. Pericarditis is quite rare with this condition.

Rheumatic fever may show pericarditis and polyarthritis, and the patient does have associated risk factors (such as being Australian Indigenous), however the other manifestations and blood results are less likely to be explained by this. For example, the mouth ulcers, and rash are less likely to be explained by rheumatic fever. As well, the anaemia and thrombocytopenia are not expected in rheumatic fever. ANA elevation is also not expected.

This is less likely to be scleroderma. While anaemia, thrombocytopenia, leukopenia and ANA elevation are all possible with scleroderma, and this condition can be a cause of pericarditis, we would expect other signs which are not present in this patient; these commonly include gastrointestinal symptoms with malabsorption. Hardening of the skin would be expected, particularly the face and the hands - these are not present here. While skin sores may happen here, scarred rashes are not expected from this condition.

Granulomatosis with polyangiitis is associated with a positive ANCA (not positive ANA). While pericarditis can occur with this condition, clinical features of GPA include productive cough, shortness of breath, and hearing loss. The full blood examination findings for this patient would be poorly explained by this condition, particularly as the white cell count is not elevated here.
learningPoints: Classifying SLE requires an ANA at a titre above 1:80, one clinical criteria, and 10 or more total points on the 2019 EULAR/ACR Classification Criteria for SLE
Pericarditis is a serious potential consequence of SLE that presents with pleuritic chest pain improving on sitting forward
Indigenous Australians have higher rates of SLE compared to non-Indigenous populations
Common haematological manifestations of SLE include anaemia, leukopenia and thrombocytopenia
A homogeneous ANA pattern suggests the presence of anti-dsDNA antibodies
references: O'Sullivan, M., McLean-Tooke, A., & Loh, R. (2013). Antinuclear antibody test. Australian Family Physician, 42(10), 718-721.
Rahman, A., & Liu, D. (2011). Pericarditis: Clinical features and management. Australian Family Physician, 40(10), 791-796.
Apostolopoulos, D., & Alberta, Y. B. H. (2013). Systemic lupus erythematosus: when to consider and management options. Australian family physician, 42(10), 696-700.

Alghareeb, R., Hussain, A., Maheshwari, M. V., Khalid, N., & Patel, P. D. (2022). Cardiovascular Complications in Systemic Lupus Erythematosus. Cureus, 14(7), e26671. https://doi.org/10.7759/cureus.26671

Vincent, F. B., Bourke, P., Morand, E. F., Mackay, F., & Bossingham, D. (2013). Focus on systemic lupus erythematosus in Indigenous Australians: towards a better understanding of autoimmune diseases. Internal Medicine Journal, 43(3), 227-234.

Petri M, Orbai A-M, Alarcón GS, et al. Derivation and validation of the Systemic Lupus International Collaborating Clinics classification criteria for systemic lupus erythematosus. Arthritis Rheum 2012;64:2677–86. Search PubMed
Wielosz, E., & Majdan, M. (2020). Haematological abnormalities in systemic sclerosis. Reumatologia, 58(3), 162–166. https://doi.org/10.5114/reum.2020.96655

Carsons SE, Patel BC. Sjogren Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Apr 3]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK431049/

Aringer, M., Costenbader, K., Daikh, D., Brinks, R., Mosca, M., Ramsey-Goldman, R., Smolen, J. S., Wofsy, D., Boumpas, D. T., Kamen, D. L., Jayne, D., Cervera, R., Costedoat-Chalumeau, N., Diamond, B., Gladman, D. D., Hahn, B., Hiepe, F., Jacobsen, S., Khanna, D., Lerstrøm, K., … Johnson, S. R. (2019). 2019 European League Against Rheumatism/American College of Rheumatology Classification Criteria for Systemic Lupus Erythematosus. Arthritis & rheumatology (Hoboken, N.J.), 71(9), 1400–1412. https://doi.org/10.1002/art.40930
